content.text: pattern places a human and an AI agent in an alternating turn loop over a shared, versioned work document. Unlike
(which lets the agent run fully and only alerts on anomalies), the collaborative pattern is about
: both parties contribute meaningful increments to the same artefact, taking turns, each building on the other's last contribution.

Typical use-cases include co-writing long-form documents (product specs, RFCs, research reports), iterative code generation (where the human adds tests or changes the API signature and the agent fills in the implementation), and structured knowledge extraction (where the human annotates raw text and the agent expands each annotation into a canonical form).

attrs.alt: Collaborative Agent human-agent turn loop diagram
attrs.title: Collaborative Agent: Human-Agent Turn Loop

content.text: The core of the pattern is a loop that alternates control between the human and the agent. The loop runs until a
, an empty turn, or an explicit finish flag) or a maximum turn count is reached.

import anthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

@dataclass
class CollabSession:
    goal: str
    doc: str = ""                    # shared versioned work document
    history: list[dict] = field(default_factory=list)
    turn: int = 0
    last_human_edit: str = ""
    last_agent_output: str = ""

def run_agent_turn(session: CollabSession, system_prompt: str) -> str:
    """Send the diff of the human's last edit plus the full doc to the agent."""
    diff_block = (
        f"Human just edited the document. Their change:\n{session.last_human_edit}\n\n"
        if session.last_human_edit else ""
    )
    user_msg = (
        f"{diff_block}"
        f"Current document:\n{session.doc}\n\n"
        f"Continue the document — draft the next logical section. "
        f"Goal: {session.goal}"
    )
    session.history.append({"role": "user", "content": user_msg})
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=2048,
        system=system_prompt,
        messages=session.history,
    )
    agent_text = response.content[0].text
    session.history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": agent_text})
    session.last_agent_output = agent_text
    return agent_text

def run_human_turn(session: CollabSession) -> Optional[str]:
    """
    Pause and collect the human's edit.
    Returns None when the human signals they are done.
    """
    print(f"\n--- Agent turn {session.turn} complete ---")
    print(session.last_agent_output)
    print("\n[Your turn] Edit or extend the document (press Enter twice to finish, /done to exit):")
    lines = []
    while True:
        line = input()
        if line == "/done":
            return None
        if line == "" and lines and lines[-1] == "":
            break
        lines.append(line)
    return "\n".join(lines).strip() or None

def collaborative_loop(goal: str, max_turns: int = 20) -> str:
    system = (
        "You are a collaborative writing assistant. "
        "Extend the shared document by drafting the next logical section. "
        "Do not repeat content already present. "
        "Write in a clear, structured style — use headers where appropriate. "
        "Stop after completing one coherent section so the human can review."
    )
    session = CollabSession(goal=goal)
    # Kick off with an agent opening
    session.last_human_edit = ""
    agent_draft = run_agent_turn(session, system)
    session.doc += agent_draft + "\n\n"
    session.turn += 1

    while session.turn < max_turns:
        human_edit = run_human_turn(session)
        if human_edit is None:
            break
        # Persist the human's incremental edit
        session.last_human_edit = human_edit
        session.doc += human_edit + "\n\n"
        session.turn += 1

        agent_draft = run_agent_turn(session, system)
        session.doc += agent_draft + "\n\n"
        session.turn += 1

    print("\n=== Final Document ===")
    print(session.doc)
    return session.doc
The agent never sees a vague prompt like "write a document". It sees the
diff of what the human just changed
. This grounds each agent turn in the actual work done so far rather than a stale system prompt.
A naive implementation passes the entire growing document to the agent on every turn. This is correct but wastes tokens as the document grows. A better approach extracts only the
of the human's last edit and summarises older turns. Two strategies work well together:
: keep only the last N message pairs in
; older turns are dropped or summarised once.
: instead of re-sending the entire document, send only the lines the human added or changed, alongside a brief recap of the document structure.

One significant UX improvement over basic request-response is streaming the agent's output as it is generated, so the human sees it being written in real time. This reduces perceived latency and lets humans interrupt early if the agent starts going in the wrong direction.

def run_agent_turn_streaming(session: CollabSession, system_prompt: str) -> str:
    """Stream the agent response token by token; return the full text."""
    user_msg = build_agent_prompt(session, session.doc)
    session.history.append({"role": "user", "content": user_msg})

    print("\n[Agent writing...]  ", end="", flush=True)
    full_text = ""
    with client.messages.stream(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=2048,
        system=system_prompt,
        messages=session.history,
    ) as stream:
        for text in stream.text_stream:
            print(text, end="", flush=True)
            full_text += text
    print()  # newline after streaming completes

    session.history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": full_text})
    session.last_agent_output = full_text
    return full_text

# For web UIs: async streaming via Server-Sent Events
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse

app = FastAPI()

@app.post("/collab/stream")
async def stream_agent_turn(payload: dict):
    """
    Endpoint that accepts the current session state and streams the
    agent's next contribution as SSE events.
    """
    import asyncio

    session = CollabSession(
        goal=payload["goal"],
        doc=payload["doc"],
        history=payload.get("history", []),
        turn=payload.get("turn", 0),
        last_human_edit=payload.get("last_human_edit", ""),
    )
    system = (
        "You are a collaborative writing assistant. "
        "Extend the shared document — draft the next logical section."
    )
    user_msg = build_agent_prompt(session, payload.get("doc_before_edit", session.doc))
    messages = session.history + [{"role": "user", "content": user_msg}]

    async def event_generator():
        with client.messages.stream(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            max_tokens=2048,
            system=system,
            messages=messages,
        ) as stream:
            for text in stream.text_stream:
                yield f"data: {json.dumps({'delta': text})}\n\n"
        yield f"data: {json.dumps({'done': True})}\n\n"

    return StreamingResponse(event_generator(), media_type="text/event-stream")
A Working Example: Co-writing a Product Spec

Below is an end-to-end trace of a three-turn collaborative session. The goal is to produce a product requirements document for a new feature. Notice how the human's edits redirect the agent's tone and scope, and the agent faithfully incorporates those changes in its next contribution.

# Full runnable example — co-writing a PRD with two human interventions

import anthropic, textwrap
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

SYSTEM = textwrap.dedent("""
    You are a senior product manager collaborating with an engineer.
    Write concise, structured PRD sections using Markdown.
    Do not repeat content already in the document.
    After each section, stop so your collaborator can review.
""").strip()

@dataclass
class CollabSession:
    goal: str
    doc: str = ""
    history: list[dict] = field(default_factory=list)
    turn: int = 0

def agent_turn(session: CollabSession, extra_context: str = "") -> str:
    prompt = f"{extra_context}\nCurrent document:\n{session.doc}\nGoal: {session.goal}"
    session.history.append({"role": "user", "content": prompt})
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=SYSTEM,
        messages=session.history,
    )
    text = resp.content[0].text
    session.history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": text})
    session.doc += text + "\n\n"
    session.turn += 1
    return text

# --- Simulated session (normally interactive) ---

goal = "PRD for an AI-powered search bar that explains its results"
session = CollabSession(goal=goal)

# Turn 1: agent opens with Overview + Goals
draft_1 = agent_turn(session)
print("=== Agent Turn 1 ===")
print(draft_1[:600], "...\n")

# Human edits: add a hard constraint the agent missed
human_edit_1 = (
    "## Constraints\n"
    "- Must work offline (no cloud round-trips for query parsing)\n"
    "- Explanation text must be ≤ 40 words to fit the UI widget\n"
)
session.doc += human_edit_1 + "\n\n"

# Turn 2: agent sees the constraint and adds User Stories + Acceptance Criteria
draft_2 = agent_turn(
    session,
    extra_context="Human added a Constraints section. Respect those constraints in the next section.",
)
print("=== Agent Turn 2 ===")
print(draft_2[:600], "...\n")

# Human corrects a metric
human_edit_2 = (
    "## Success Metrics (revised)\n"
    "- P50 latency for explanation generation: < 300 ms (agent wrote 500 ms — too slow)\n"
    "- User satisfaction score > 4.2/5 after 30-day pilot\n"
)
session.doc += human_edit_2 + "\n\n"

# Turn 3: agent wraps up with Open Questions + Timeline
draft_3 = agent_turn(
    session,
    extra_context="Human revised Success Metrics. Close out with Open Questions and a rough Timeline.",
)
print("=== Agent Turn 3 ===")
print(draft_3[:600], "...\n")

print("\n=== Final PRD (truncated) ===")
print(session.doc[:1200], "...")
When to Use the Collaborative Agent Pattern
Choose the right HITL variant based on the autonomy-control tradeoff for your task:
The collaborative pattern is the right choice when:
and the human must steer quality throughout — not just at the end.
: the agent knows structure and language; the human knows facts, constraints, or business context.
and drift across long agent runs without human course-correction.
— writing, code review, annotation, specification — where each pass adds value rather than just fixing errors.
When the agent and human repeatedly pull in opposite directions, the session can stall or diverge. Robust implementations add two safeguards:

def detect_conflict(session: CollabSession) -> bool:
    """
    Heuristic: if the agent just overwrote a section the human added
    in the previous turn, flag a conflict.
    """
    if session.turn < 2:
        return False
    human_keywords = set(session.last_human_edit.lower().split())
    # Check if agent output removed key terms the human introduced
    agent_words = set(session.last_agent_output.lower().split())
    overlap = human_keywords & agent_words
    # If < 30% of human terms appear in agent output, likely a conflict
    if not human_keywords:
        return False
    return len(overlap) / len(human_keywords) < 0.30

def handle_conflict(session: CollabSession) -> None:
    """Inject an explicit arbitration message into the history."""
    arbitration = (
        "SYSTEM NOTE: There appears to be a direction conflict. "
        "Prioritise the human's most recent edits — they override your previous draft. "
        "Incorporate their changes fully before adding new content."
    )
    session.history.append({"role": "user", "content": arbitration})
list grows unbounded. Cap it at 20 message pairs and summarise older turns into a single system-level recap message.
: the agent regenerates entire sections rather than extending them. Fix by appending a
instruction to the system prompt and diffing before accepting agent output.
. Always include a progress gauge (% of goal met) and allow the human to exit with a
: after 10+ turns the agent forgets the original
. Re-inject the goal statement at every turn, not just the first one.
with a partial assistant message. Always wrap streaming in a try/except and commit only the full text to history.

The Collaborative Agent pattern structures human-AI interaction as an alternating turn loop over a shared, versioned work document. Both parties contribute meaningfully on each turn: the agent drafts and extends, the human reviews and steers. Key implementation elements are a

dataclass that tracks document state and message history; a diff-based context injection strategy that limits token usage as the document grows; streaming output so the human can interrupt early; and conflict detection to prevent the agent from overwriting human contributions.

Use this pattern when the task is long, iterative, and requires domain expertise from both sides — co-writing specifications, generating code with human test coverage, or extracting structured knowledge from annotated text. For tasks that are routine and well-bounded, prefer the autonomous pattern; for high-stakes irreversible actions, prefer Human-in-the-Loop.
